variables.I0: Intensity of polarised light incident on analyser (W/m²)
variables.I: Intensity of light transmitted through analyser (W/m²)
variables.theta: Angle between polariser and analyser pass axes (rad or °)

locked_facts: Malus's law: I = I₀ cos²θ, where θ is the angle between polariser and analyser pass axes
When unpolarised light passes through a polariser, the transmitted intensity = I₀/2 (half the incident intensity)
Brewster's angle: tan θ_B = n₂/n₁ — at this angle, the reflected ray is completely polarised
At Brewster's angle, the reflected and refracted rays are perpendicular to each other (θ_B + θ_r = 90°)
Only transverse waves can be polarised — longitudinal waves (like sound) cannot be polarised
Crossed polarisers (θ = 90°) transmit zero intensity: I = I₀ cos²90° = 0
truth_statements: Polarisation is the phenomenon of restricting the vibration of a wave to a single plane, and it conclusively proves that light is a transverse wave
Malus's law I = I₀cos²θ describes how the intensity of polarised light varies as it passes through a second polariser (analyser) at angle θ
An unpolarised light beam consists of electric field oscillations in all directions perpendicular to propagation — a polariser selects only one direction
At Brewster's angle, the reflected light is completely polarised with its electric field parallel to the reflecting surface
Polaroid sunglasses work by blocking horizontally polarised glare reflected from roads and water surfaces

aha_moment.what_to_show: Rotate the analyser from 0° to 90°. At 0°, full intensity passes. At 45°, exactly half passes (cos²45° = 0.5). At 90°, zero light passes — complete extinction. Then insert a third polariser at 45° between two crossed polarisers — light reappears! This defies the intuition that adding a filter should only reduce light.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Intensity meter reading I₀ → I₀/2 → 0 as analyser rotates, then the dramatic reappearance of light when a third polariser is inserted at 45° between crossed polarisers
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see that polarisation is NOT about blocking light like a curtain — it rotates the vibration plane, so a 45° filter can 'unlock' light that two crossed filters blocked

simulation_states.label: Unpolarised light
simulation_states.description: A beam of unpolarised light is shown with electric field arrows vibrating in all directions perpendicular to the propagation direction. The arrows change orientation randomly. Intensity = I₀.
simulation_states.emphasis: Unpolarised light has E-field oscillations in all transverse directions — no preferred plane of vibration

simulation_states.label: After passing through polariser
simulation_states.description: The light passes through a polariser. Only the component of E-field along the pass axis survives. The transmitted light vibrates in one plane only. Intensity reduces to I₀/2.
simulation_states.emphasis: The polariser selects ONE vibration direction — transmitted intensity is exactly half the incident unpolarised intensity

simulation_states.label: Malus's law with analyser
simulation_states.description: A second polariser (analyser) is placed after the first. As the angle θ between their pass axes varies, the transmitted intensity follows I = I₀cos²θ. At θ = 0° full transmission; at θ = 90° complete extinction. Intensity vs θ curve displayed.
simulation_states.emphasis: I = I₀cos²θ — this is Malus's law. At 90° (crossed polarisers), NO light passes through.

simulation_states.label: Interactive exploration
simulation_states.description: Student rotates the analyser angle and observes intensity change in real time. A Brewster's angle mode lets the student adjust incidence angle on a glass surface and observe when reflected light becomes completely polarised. Optional third polariser insertion between crossed polarisers.
simulation_states.emphasis: Rotate the analyser to verify cos² law. Find Brewster's angle for glass (n=1.5 → θ_B ≈ 56.3°).

common_misconceptions.belief: Polarisation proves light is a longitudinal wave because it blocks some vibrations
common_misconceptions.correction: Polarisation is possible ONLY for transverse waves — it is in fact the strongest proof that light is transverse. Longitudinal waves (like sound) vibrate along the propagation direction and cannot be restricted to a single plane.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show transverse wave passing through a slit (polariser) vs longitudinal wave going through — the longitudinal wave is unaffected by the slit orientation

common_misconceptions.belief: A polariser blocks exactly half the light because it absorbs 50% of the photons randomly
common_misconceptions.correction: A polariser transmits the component of the E-field along its pass axis. For unpolarised light, the average value of cos²θ over all angles is 1/2, giving I = I₀/2. It is not random absorption — it is selective transmission of one vibration component. Each photon has a cos²θ probability of being transmitted depending on its polarisation angle.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show E-field vectors at various angles hitting the polariser — only the component along the pass axis survives, and averaging gives exactly half

legend.meaning: Angle between polariser and analyser pass axes
Refractive indices of the two media
